common_name: Sun Sugar Cherry Tomato (Extremely Sweet Orange F1 Cherry Tomato, Japan Tokita Seed)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Sun Sugar is an indeterminate cherry tomato that benefits from caging or staking and grows throughout the season. In zone 9, transplant March–April for summer production. Sun Sugar's exceptional crack resistance — unusual in cherry tomatoes, which commonly split at the stem end when they absorb excess water rapidly after dry periods — makes it more practical in zone 9's irregular summer rainfall than crack-prone cherry types. The fruits are orange to golden-orange (slightly deeper than the orange of Sungold under some growing conditions — though Sun Sugar and Sungold are sometimes confused in the US market, they are distinct varieties), and the sweetness consistently registers among the highest of any commercial cherry tomato variety. The flavor profile combines intense sweetness with mild acidity and a complex, fruity tomato character. Harvest clusters when uniformly orange.

directions: Start seeds indoors at 70–85°F; transplant after last frost and stake or cage. Harvest clusters when uniformly orange. Sun Sugar is an F1 hybrid cherry tomato developed by Tokita Seeds of Japan — one of the premier vegetable seed breeding companies in the world — and became widely popular in North America through distribution by companies including Park Seed and Renee's Garden Seeds, where it is sometimes sold under the Park Seed exclusive name 'Sun Sugar.' Sun Sugar consistently ranks among the highest-rated cherry tomato varieties for sweetness and flavor in home garden and market garden trials — its Brix (sugar content) measurements frequently exceed 10 (compared to 6–8 for standard slicing tomatoes), and the flavor combines this exceptional sweetness with mild acidity and what tasters frequently describe as a complex, almost citrus-tropical fruitiness distinct from standard red cherry types. The color — orange to golden-orange, slightly more orange than Sungold (which is from Takii Seeds, a different Japanese company) — is part of the flavor differentiation: orange cherry tomatoes in this category contain different proportions of carotenoids than red types, often with higher beta-carotene content and lower lycopene, contributing to their distinctively fruity, mild-acid flavor. Sun Sugar's crack resistance in the field is a practical advantage that Sungold (its main competitor) has been criticized for lacking — the characteristic of splitting when water uptake increases rapidly after drought periods plagues many cherry tomato varieties, and Sun Sugar's better resistance reduces the frustration of finding split fruit after rain events.
sources: Tokita Seeds Japan — Sun Sugar cherry tomato F1 hybrid development
Park Seed — Sun Sugar cherry tomato performance and sweetness documentation
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Orange cherry tomato variety comparison and Brix data

common_name: Sweet 100 Cherry Tomato (Classic Ultra-Productive Sweet Red Indeterminate Cherry)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Sweet 100 is a vigorous indeterminate plant that grows very tall (6–8+ feet) and produces enormous clusters of cherry tomatoes throughout the season — staking is essential, with minimum 6-foot stakes or a sturdy cage recommended. In zone 9, transplant March–April for early summer production that continues until fall or first frost. The '100' in the name references the extremely large number of tomatoes per cluster — a single cluster can hold 50–100 or more cherry tomatoes, and a well-established plant in zone 9's long season produces continuously from June through November with minimal stopping. Sweet 100 is somewhat susceptible to cracking in irregular watering conditions (a common complaint), but the sheer productivity and classic sweet-acid cherry tomato flavor have maintained its popularity for decades.

directions: Start seeds indoors; transplant after last frost and provide minimum 6-foot stake. Harvest clusters when uniformly red. Sweet 100 is one of the most famous and historically significant cherry tomato varieties in American home gardening — an F1 hybrid first introduced in the early 1980s that set a new standard for cherry tomato productivity and established the category of 'sweet cherry tomato' as distinct from larger slicing tomato types. Before Sweet 100, cherry tomatoes were typically valued for novelty and ease of snacking rather than for the combination of extremely high productivity and genuinely sweet, complex flavor that Sweet 100 provided. The variety produces long, branching clusters with an extraordinary number of fruits per cluster — sometimes 50–100 per cluster, as the name implies — with uniformly round, 1-inch red fruits and a flavor that combines sweetness with the right amount of acidity to create what many gardeners consider the definitive cherry tomato flavor. The flavor balance of Sweet 100 — sweet but not cloying, with genuine tomato acid and complexity — made it the reference standard against which subsequent cherry varieties were judged for a generation. Sweet 100's high vitamin C content (documented to be substantially higher than larger slicing tomatoes) and prolific production made it popular both as a garden plant and as a crop for farmers markets where volume production of cherry tomatoes was economically important. Several improved varieties building on Sweet 100's genetics followed, including Sweet Million (improved crack resistance), Supersweet 100 (same name but improved disease package), and others — but the original Sweet 100 remains widely grown and available.
sources: W. Atlee Burpee — Sweet 100 cherry tomato historical introduction documentation
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Sweet cherry tomato variety comparison and productivity data
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cherry Tomato Variety Selection for the Gulf South

common_name: Black Grape Tomato (Dark Purple-Black Grape Cluster Tomato with Complex Rich Flavor)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Black Grape is a compact to medium-sized indeterminate cherry tomato that produces clusters of elongated grape-shaped fruits. In zone 9, transplant March–April. Black Grape is one of the most distinctive cherry tomatoes visually — the fruits are uniformly dark purple-black when ripe, with no streaking or green shoulders, and in good growing conditions may have a slight sheen to the skin. The dark color comes from high anthocyanin content in the skin (the same pigments that make black tomatoes distinctive), which combines with lycopene in the flesh to produce the characteristic complex, sweet-rich flavor. The anthocyanin content of Black Grape and similar dark tomatoes is higher in plants grown in high-light conditions, so zone 9's full sun intensifies both the color and the antioxidant content compared to lower-light growing environments.

directions: Start seeds indoors; transplant after last frost and stake. Harvest when fruits are uniformly deep purple-black throughout the cluster. Black Grape Tomato is a compact indeterminate variety producing clusters of small, oval to elongated-grape-shaped fruits that ripen to a very dark purple-black color — one of the darkest small-fruited tomato varieties commercially available. The dark coloration results from high anthocyanin content in the skin (the same class of pigments responsible for the color of blueberries, eggplant, and purple cabbage) combined with the lycopene that gives tomatoes their characteristic red color. When anthocyanins are expressed strongly in tomato skin and lycopene is present in the flesh, the result is a dark fruit that appears almost black in good light. This anthocyanin content has nutritional significance — anthocyanins are potent antioxidants, and dark-fruited tomatoes contain higher concentrations than red or yellow types. The flavor of Black Grape combines the sweetness of a cherry tomato with additional complexity and depth — a rich, almost wine-like quality that distinguishes dark tomatoes from standard red types. The texture is firm and the grape shape (elongated, 1–1.5 inch) makes Black Grape practical for packing and transporting without the splitting that affects rounder cherry types. This firmness combined with the dramatically dark color makes Black Grape particularly popular at farmers markets and in restaurant settings where visual distinction is valued. The variety's compact growth habit relative to Sweet 100 and similar indeterminate cherry types makes it easier to manage in average-sized garden spaces.
sources: Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Black Grape tomato dark cherry variety notes
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Dark-fruited cherry tomato variety comparison
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Cherry and Grape Tomato Variety Evaluation

common_name: Currant Tomato (Wild-Species Ancestor Tomato with Intensely Complex Flavor, Tiny Fruits)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; Currant Tomato (Solanum pimpinellifolium) is a wild-species tomato or near-wild type — the wild ancestor of cultivated tomatoes — producing tiny (1/4 to 1/2 inch) fruits on vigorous, sprawling vines that can spread 4–6 feet or more. In zone 9, transplant March–April; S. pimpinellifolium is more heat and drought tolerant than cultivated tomatoes and continues producing through Gulf Coast summers that stress standard varieties. The vines behave somewhat like perennials in zone 9b, potentially surviving mild winters to produce again the following year. Provide a large cage or trellis — the vines are more vigorous than most garden tomatoes. The tiny fruits are produced in very large clusters and ripen red, producing a seemingly endless supply of intensely-flavored small tomatoes through the season. This species is extremely easy to grow and extremely productive in zone 9's warm conditions.

directions: Start seeds indoors or direct sow after last frost; provide large cage or trellis. Harvest clusters when red throughout. Solanum pimpinellifolium, the currant tomato, is the wild progenitor species of modern cultivated tomatoes — one of the wild species native to western South America (Ecuador and Peru) from which Solanum lycopersicum was domesticated thousands of years ago by indigenous peoples of the Americas. The currant tomato has been preserved as a distinct variety in seed collections and home gardens and represents a connection to the pre-domestication history of the tomato: the tiny fruits (roughly the size of a pea or large currant, giving the species its common name) look nothing like modern beefsteak or even cherry tomatoes, but they represent the ancestral form before artificial selection increased fruit size dramatically over millennia. Despite or because of this wild ancestry, the flavor of currant tomatoes is frequently described as intense, complex, and distinctly more flavorful than larger cultivated varieties — a small amount of wild material containing concentrated flavor compounds produces a dramatically different eating experience from a standard cherry tomato. The flavor is balanced sweet-acid with herbal, almost earthy notes that have led food writers to describe currant tomatoes as tasting 'more like a real tomato' than most cultivated varieties. The species is also significantly more heat, drought, and disease tolerant than cultivated tomatoes — traits expected from a wild plant that survived without human irrigation or disease management. In zone 9, S. pimpinellifolium plants perform exceptionally well through the full summer growing season and can sometimes persist as short-lived perennials in the mildest locations.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Solanum pimpinellifolium currant tomato wild-species documentation
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Currant tomato wild species variety notes
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Solanum pimpinellifolium wild tomato germplasm collection

common_name: First Lady Tomato (Disease-Resistant Hybrid Slicing Tomato, Classic Backyard Producer)

planting.note: Start indoors 4–6 weeks before transplanting; First Lady is an indeterminate slicing tomato with a broad disease resistance package: resistance to Verticillium wilt (V), two races of Fusarium wilt (F, F2), nematodes (N), tobacco mosaic virus (T), and Alternaria (A). In zone 9, transplant March–April. The comprehensive disease resistance makes First Lady particularly practical for zone 9's warm, humid conditions where soilborne pathogens (Verticillium, Fusarium) and nematodes significantly limit yields in susceptible varieties. First Lady was developed specifically for home garden production and has remained one of the most widely sold tomato transplants at garden centers throughout the southeastern United States for decades. Fruits are medium (8–10 oz), round, smooth-shouldered, uniformly red, and produced abundantly through the season. The flavor is balanced — typical of the reliable but moderate flavor of disease-resistant F1 hybrids rather than the complex intensity of heirloom types.

directions: Start seeds indoors; transplant after last frost. Stake or cage. Harvest when uniformly red and gives to slight pressure. First Lady Tomato is an F1 hybrid indeterminate slicing tomato developed by Seminis Vegetable Seeds (now Bayer Vegetable Seeds) with a comprehensive disease resistance package that has made it one of the most reliably productive backyard tomato varieties in the humid southeastern United States. The 'VFFNTAs' resistance package (Verticillium wilt, two races of Fusarium wilt, Nematodes, Tobacco Mosaic Virus, Alternaria) addresses virtually every major soil-borne and airborne pathogen that limits tomato production in the Gulf South — in humid zone 9 conditions where tomato plants commonly succumb to one or more of these diseases before the season ends, First Lady's resistance allows plants to remain productive through the summer. The variety's profile — medium-sized (8–10 oz) red tomatoes on vigorous indeterminate plants with the comprehensive disease package — represents a classic template for southeastern home garden tomato production that prioritizes plant health and extended production over extreme fruit size or complex flavor. First Lady was marketed and sold through retail garden center channels for decades and became one of the most recognized tomato variety names in southeastern garden retail, appearing consistently on transplant packs in spring garden center displays across the Gulf South. For zone 9 gardeners dealing with nematode pressure (very common in sandy Gulf Coast soils) or Fusarium wilt (endemic in soils with tomato production history), First Lady's 'N' and 'F' resistances provide practical protection that keeps plants alive and producing when susceptible varieties decline.
sources: Seminis Vegetable Seeds — First Lady tomato F1 hybrid disease resistance package documentation
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tomato Variety Recommendations for Gulf South Home Gardens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Disease-Resistant Tomato Variety Selection for Florida
